claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start with the basics:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you looking to claim Child Benefit for?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old and I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few more questions:

**Do you currently live in the UK with Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK and Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you. 

**Are you responsible for Alex's care?** For example, do you provide for his day-to-day needs such as food, clothes, and general upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am responsible for Alex. I look after him and provide for all his day-to-day needs - his food, clothes, and everything he needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you for confirming that.

Just a few more questions to complete the assessment:

**Is Alex currently in hospital, in care, or living in any residential accommodation provided by the local council?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in care for a couple of months, but it's a temporary situation.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. This is important information as it can affect your Child Benefit eligibility.

**Can you tell me more specifically how long Alex has been in care? Has it been more than 8 weeks?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been exactly 8 weeks that he's been in care.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex has been in care for exactly 8 weeks, I have a few more questions:

**Does Alex spend any regular time at home with you? For example, does he come home for at least 24 hours per week, or spend any nights at home regularly?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex is in the care facility full-time. He doesn't come home regularly - it's a full-time placement.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Thank you for providing that information.

Just to confirm a few final details:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, such as a relative or the care facility?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

One last question to complete my assessment:

**Are you making any financial contributions toward Alex's upkeep while he's in care?** For example, are you regularly spending money on things like clothes, presents, pocket money, or contributing financially to provide him with somewhere to live? (This would need to be at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment, which is currently £25.60 per week for the eldest/only child.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not currently contributing financially towards Alex while he's in care. The care facility is covering his needs at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Complete

Based on the information you've provided, I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

**Here's why:**

While you would normally qualify for Child Benefit for Alex (as he's 8 years old, lives in the UK with you, and you're responsible for his care), the situation has changed because he's been in care.

According to the Child Benefit rules:
- You can continue to receive Child Benefit for **8 weeks** after your child goes into care
- Since Alex has now been in care for exactly 8 weeks, this grace period has ended
- After 8 weeks, you can only continue receiving Child Benefit if certain exceptions apply:
  - If the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home, OR
  - If you're regularly making financial contributions toward their upkeep (at least equal to the Child Benefit amount)

Since Alex is in full-time care, doesn't come home regularly, and you're not currently making financial contributions toward his upkeep, none of these exceptions apply to your situation.

**Important to note:** If your circumstances change - for example, if Alex starts coming home regularly or you begin contributing financially toward his care - you should contact HMRC as you may become eligible again. Also, if Alex returns home permanently, you can reclaim Child Benefit at that time.

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and normally lives in the UK with the claimant, which would typically meet the basic eligibility criteria. However, Alex has been in care for exactly 8 weeks on a full-time basis. According to the rules, Child Benefit payments are affected if a child goes into care for more than 8 weeks. While you can continue to receive Child Benefit for 8 weeks after a child goes into care, after this 8-week period you are usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit unless certain exceptions apply. The main exception that could apply is if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home, but Alex does not come home regularly. Another exception is if you are making financial contributions toward Alex's upkeep equal to at least the Child Benefit amount, but you confirmed you are not currently making such contributions. Since Alex has now been in care for 8 weeks, the 8-week grace period has ended, and no exceptions apply to your situation, you are no longer eligible for Child Benefit for Alex at this time.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 8), who has been in full-time care for exactly 8 weeks. While the claimant normally lives with Alex in the UK and was responsible for his care, the 8-week grace period for children in care has now ended. Since Alex does not spend at least 24 hours per week at home and the claimant is not making financial contributions toward his upkeep, none of the exceptions that would allow Child Benefit to continue apply. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
